title: 4 Processing Incoming Clearing Items
body_text:
4	Processing Incoming Clearing Items
4.1	Overview of Processing Incoming Clearing Items
Incoming Clearing Items are drawn directly on the credit union or on a member of the credit union. Once the item is cashed or deposited, it is sent through the Clearing to Central 1 and debited to the credit union.
Qualified Canadian dollar items are classified as In-Province or Out-of-Province Items depending on the regional settlement point through which the items are processed. Qualified US dollar items are processed, settled, and reported separately from Canadian dollar items. The In-Province and Out-of-Province designation applies only to Canadian dollar items.
If Central 1 cannot process an item on its imaging equipment, it is considered an Unqualified Item. Credit unions can download unqualified items from the File and Report Exchange.
Credit unions use the Online Return System (ORS) to view, validate, return, and reinstate Incoming Clearing Items.
4.2	Qualified Canadian Dollar Clearing Items
Canadian dollar Incoming Clearing Items are known as In-Province or Out-of-Province Items depending on the regional settlement point through which they are processed. Items cleared through Vancouver, Montreal, Toronto, or Halifax are considered In-Province Items. Items cleared though Calgary or Winnipeg are considered Out-of-Province Items.
A list of Qualified In-Province Items is provided on the CLI report. Qualified Out-of-Province Items are listed on the CLO report. See Chapter 8, Incoming Clearing Reports, for more information on these reports.
Images of these items are available on ORS. See the Online Return System (ORS) Part for procedures on researching, viewing, printing, and returning Qualified Incoming Clearing Items.
For information on settlement to the credit union芒聙聶s Central 1 account and settlement to the members芒聙聶 accounts, see Chapter 6, Settlement of Incoming Clearing Items.
4.3	Qualified US Dollar Clearing Items
US dollar Incoming Clearing Items are processed in the same way as Canadian dollar items unless otherwise noted. US dollar Incoming Clearing Items can be
cheques or drafts drawn on your credit union,
cheques drawn on your member芒聙聶s US dollar account, or
cheques drawn on your member芒聙聶s Canadian dollar account (but the cheque is payable in US dollars).
US dollar Incoming Clearing Items are processed, settled, and reported separately from Canadian dollar items. The In-Province and Out-of-Province designation doesn芒聙聶t apply to US dollar items. Images of these items are available on ORS. See the Online Return System (ORS) Part for procedures on researching, viewing, printing, and returning Qualified Incoming Clearing Items.
A list of US dollar Qualified Items is provided on the CLUS report. See Chapter 7, Incoming Clearing Reports, for more information on this report.
Unqualified US dollar cheques are processed the same way as Canadian dollar Unqualified Items. See Section 4.4, Unqualified Clearing Items, for information.
For information on settlement to the credit union芒聙聶s Central 1 account and settlement to the members芒聙聶 accounts, see Chapter 6, Settlement of Incoming Clearing Items.
US Dollar Cheques Negotiated in the US
US dollar cheques that are drawn on the credit union or a member of the credit union can be negotiated in the US. Although these cheques are denominated in US dollars, they are considered foreign items within the US Clearing system and are treated differently than domestic US dollar cheques.
Foreign cheques are out-sorted by the negotiating financial institution and forwarded to the Federal Reserve or a Canadian correspondent bank. In turn, the Federal Reserve or the Canadian correspondent bank delivers the foreign cheques to a point of entry bank in Canada for processing within the Canadian Clearing system. Consequently, the negotiating bank incurs additional costs and handling charges to process these items.
The Federal Reserve has procedures for US financial institutions to follow to settle cross-border cheques with Canadian financial institutions. However, it is up to each bank to determine whether it accepts foreign cheques on deposit. Most of these cheques are negotiated and cleared properly without problems. If the acceptability of a US dollar cheque drawn on a Canadian financial institution is in question, credit union staff can suggest a US dollar draft or wire as an alternate payment method.
4.4	Unqualified Clearing Items
If a Financial Institution isn芒聙聶t set up for image exchange with Central 1, the Financial Institution芒聙聶s Incoming Clearing Items are processed by Central 1芒聙聶s sorting equipment. If Central芒聙聶s sorting equipment rejects a Canadian or US dollar Incoming Clearing Item that cannot be corrected and imaged, it is considered an Unqualified Item (e.g., an item with a damaged or missing MICR line).
If Central 1 cannot determine which credit union the item is drawn on, it is returned to the negotiating financial institution. The credit union doesn芒聙聶t receive settlement for these Unqualified Items, and they aren芒聙聶t included on any Central 1 reports.
However, if Central 1 is able to determine which credit union an Unqualified Item is drawn on, Central 1 creates an Unqualified/Return Cheque Adjustment Slip and debits the credit union. See Figure 1. Download a copy of the Unqualified Item and the corresponding adjustment slip from the File and Report Exchange. Copies of the Unqualified Item and corresponding adjustment slip are available in the Clearing folder. Go to the Clearing folder and select the Unqualified subfolder.
Figure 1: Unqualified/Return Cheque Adjustment Slip
Unqualified Items aren芒聙聶t included in the
DCPA/QCPA Files (Ontario Only),
DREC/QREC Files (BC Only),
XDREC/XQREC Files (BC and Ontario),
Online Return System (ORS), or
the clearing reports (CLI, CLO, CLUS).
Unqualified Items aren芒聙聶t debited to the credit union芒聙聶s account with the Qualified Items. Instead, the total on each Unqualified/Return Cheque Adjustment Slip is debited to the credit union芒聙聶s account and is displayed on the statement as 芒聙聹Unqualified Clearing芒聙聺 for Ontario and as code 芒聙聹UC芒聙聺 (Unqualified Clearing) for BC. Multiple Unqualified Items may be included on an Unqualified/Return Cheque Adjustment Slip. Manually debit the member芒聙聶s account when the Unqualified Item is received at the credit union.
Related Information
Chapter 7
, Incoming Clearing Reports
4.5	Chargeback Items
If the Drawee financial institution dishonours and returns an item that the credit union deposited, it is known as a Chargeback. Images of these items are available on ORS. See the Online Return System (ORS) Part for information on how to retrieve Chargebacks on ORS.
Upon receipt of a Chargeback, debit the account of the member who deposited the cheque.
Chargebacks for Canadian and US dollar items are included in the clearing reports (CLI, CLO, and CLUS). See Chapter 7, Incoming Clearing Reports, for more information on these reports and to view samples.
All Chargebacks will be available on ORS one business day after the date the item is received by Central 1.
If a Chargeback is sent to the credit union in error, follow the manual return process to redirect Chargeback to the negotiating financial institution. See the Dishonour and Return of Cheques Part for a description of the manual return process.
Class A credit unions in BC and Ontario can refer to the Payment Services Class A Fee Schedule, Form 2725, to view Central 1芒聙聶s chargeback fees.
Related Information
Payment Services Class A Fee Schedule,
Form 2725
(available to Class A credit unions in BC and Ontario)
4.6	Incorrectly Amount-Encoded Items and Cleared in Wrong Currency Items
If the credit union receives an Incoming Clearing Item that is incorrectly amount-encoded or cleared in the wrong currency, return the item to the negotiating financial institution via ORS. The CPA doesn芒聙聶t allow financial institutions to certify dishonoured cheques. However, the negotiating financial institution can reclear the item even if it isn芒聙聶t certified.
Note:	Items that are incorrectly amount-encoded cannot be returned to the negotiating financial institution if the difference is $20 or less.
Return the item as soon as possible and within the 90-day CPA time frame. The CPA Rules don芒聙聶t require credit unions to hold these items for five days before returning them.
Once the cheque is returned to the negotiating financial institution, the negotiating financial institution can reclear the cheque with the correct currency or amount. When the cheque is recleared, it is received with the credit union芒聙聶s Incoming Clearing Items. An image of the cheque appears on ORS and the cheque is included in the Clearing reports (CLI, CLO, or CLUS).
4.7	Duplicate Payments
If a cheque drawn on a member is deposited twice, the credit union can view these items on ORS. As part of the overnight processing, Central 1 compares all items drawn on the credit union against items drawn on the credit union in the previous 180 days. If a duplicate payment is detected, both items are flagged as suspected duplicate items.
ORS allows users to search for suspect items and displays cheques that may be duplicates. A cheque is considered a duplicate if it has the same cheque number, account number, Transit Number, and amount as another cheque. See the Online Return System (ORS) Part for procedures on searching for cheques and identifying suspected duplicate items.
If a duplicate payment is identified, return it on ORS within 90 calendar days of the clearing date.
Note:	Central 1 cannot guarantee that all duplicate payments will be identified. This service is provided on a best-efforts basis.
END
